explicit_text: I was born in Virginia and grew up in Louisville, Kentucky. I studied at the University of Kentucky where Guy Davenport was my main mentor. Over time, I became deeply involved in environmental writing, especially around mountaintop removal mining, which culminated in my book Lost Mountain. I never considered myself an activist in the traditional sense but rather someone who writes to bring attention to environmental issues. My journey in academia was unconventional, and I emphasize storytelling as my main tool for advocacy.

I never considered myself an activist in the traditional sense but rather someone who writes to bring attention to environmental issues. My journey in academia was unconventional, and I emphasize storytelling as my main tool for advocacy.

entity: Kentucky Climate Change Oral History Project
explicit_text: I was interviewed for the Kentucky Climate Change Oral History Project at my home in Versailles, Kentucky on July 26, 2023. This project collects stories about climate change and environmental issues in Kentucky, providing a platform for voices like mine to share experiences and insights. It was conducted by Zada Komara, a fellow faculty member at the University of Kentucky, which made the conversation feel collegial and meaningful.
implicit_text: I was interviewed for a climate change oral history project focused on Kentucky at my home in Versailles, Kentucky on July 26, 2023. This project collects stories about climate change and environmental issues in Kentucky, providing a platform for voices like mine to share experiences and insights. It was conducted by a fellow faculty member at the university where I teach, which made the conversation feel collegial and meaningful.

explicit_text: I live in Versailles, Kentucky, and it was at my home here that I was interviewed for the Kentucky Climate Change Oral History Project in 2023. Versailles is where I balance my writing and daily life, like painting my deck on hot days. It’s a place that grounds me while I engage with broader environmental issues affecting the state and region.
implicit_text: I live in a small city in Kentucky, and it was at my home here that I was interviewed for a climate change oral history project in 2023. cues: Kentucky Climate Change Oral History Project

explicit_text: I studied at the University of Kentucky where I earned my undergraduate and master's degrees, though I never pursued a PhD. Guy Davenport, a key mentor there, taught me a lot about writing at the sentence level. Later, I became a faculty member at UK, working my way up from lecturer to assistant professor, largely supported by Dwight Billings. The university has been central to my academic and writing career, even as I critiqued its stance on coal mining.
implicit_text: I studied at a major public university in Kentucky where I earned my undergraduate and master's degrees, though I never pursued a PhD. A key mentor there taught me a lot about writing at the sentence level. Later, I became a faculty member at that same institution, working my way up from lecturer to assistant professor, largely supported by a senior colleague. The university has been central to my academic and writing career, even as I critiqued its stance on coal mining.

explicit_text: I was born in Virginia and spent part of my childhood there, especially in Tidewater where my grandfather was a country preacher. I have fond memories of spending time outdoors with him, canoeing and exploring the woods and water. This early exposure to nature in Virginia helped shape my lifelong connection to the natural world, despite growing up mostly in an urban Louisville environment.
implicit_text: I was born in a Mid-Atlantic state and spent part of my childhood there, especially in the Tidewater region where my grandfather was a country preacher. I have fond memories of spending time outdoors with him, canoeing and exploring the woods and water. This early exposure to nature in that state helped shape my lifelong connection to the natural world, despite growing up mostly in an urban Kentucky environment.

explicit_text: Though I was born in Virginia, I grew up in Louisville, Kentucky, which was a mostly urban and suburban environment. My family life there didn’t involve much camping or outdoor activity, but it was still home. Louisville represents the contrast in my life between urban living and my pull toward nature, which I attribute to my grandfather’s influence.
implicit_text: Though I was born in Virginia, I grew up in a large city in Kentucky, which was a mostly urban and suburban environment. My family life there didn’t involve much camping or outdoor activity, but it was still home. This city represents the contrast in my life between urban living and my pull toward nature, which I attribute to my grandfather’s influence.

explicit_text: Guy Davenport was my main mentor at the University of Kentucky and taught me a great deal about writing, especially how to craft sentences carefully. He was a heavy smoker and unfortunately died of lung cancer. After his death, his body was donated to the Markey Cancer Center, and a tree was dedicated to him at the Arboretum, where I later met Wendell Berry. His mentorship was foundational to my development as a writer.
implicit_text: My main mentor at the university in Kentucky taught me a great deal about writing, especially how to craft sentences carefully. He was a heavy smoker and unfortunately died of lung cancer. After his death, his body was donated to a cancer research center, and a tree was dedicated to him at a local arboretum, where I later met another important environmental writer. His mentorship was foundational to my development as a writer.

explicit_text: The Markey Cancer Center is where Guy Davenport's body was donated after he died of lung cancer. This connection was meaningful to me because it led to a tree being dedicated to him at the Arboretum. The center symbolizes both the loss of a mentor and the continuation of his legacy in a place of healing and remembrance.
implicit_text: The cancer research center is where my mentor's body was donated after he died of lung cancer. This connection was meaningful to me because it led to a tree being dedicated to him at the arboretum. The center symbolizes both the loss of a mentor and the continuation of his legacy in a place of healing and remembrance.

explicit_text: The Arboretum is where a tree was dedicated to my mentor Guy Davenport after his death. It was also the place where I met Wendell Berry, which was a significant moment in my life. The Arboretum serves as a symbolic and physical space connecting mentorship, nature, and community for me.
implicit_text: The arboretum is where a tree was dedicated to my mentor after his death. It was also the place where I met an influential environmental writer, which was a significant moment in my life. The arboretum serves as a symbolic and physical space connecting mentorship, nature, and community for me.

explicit_text: I met Wendell Berry at the Arboretum the day we dedicated a tree to Guy Davenport. Wendell had read my article Death of a Mountain and was organizing a tour for writers to fly over coal fields and talk to affected communities, inspired by John Dos Passos' poverty tours. I joined that tour and worked alongside Wendell and others like Teri Blanton and Dave Cooper to bring attention to mountaintop removal mining. Wendell became a mentor and collaborator in my environmental activism and writing.
implicit_text: I met a renowned environmentalist at the arboretum the day we dedicated a tree to my mentor. He had read my article about mountaintop removal and was organizing a tour for writers to fly over coal fields and talk to affected communities, inspired by a 1930s poverty tour. I joined that tour and worked alongside him and others to bring attention to mountaintop removal mining. He became a mentor and collaborator in my environmental activism and writing.

explicit_text: Death of a Mountain was an article I wrote for Harper's Magazine that brought attention to mountaintop removal mining. Wendell Berry read it and invited me to join a writers' tour over the coal fields to witness the impact firsthand. This piece helped catalyze a larger movement of writers and activists focused on coal mining's environmental damage. It was an important early work in my environmental writing career.
implicit_text: An article I wrote for a major magazine brought attention to mountaintop removal mining. This environmentalist read it and invited me to join a writers' tour over the coal fields to witness the impact firsthand. This piece helped catalyze a larger movement of writers and activists focused on coal mining's environmental damage. It was an important early work in my environmental writing career.

explicit_text: Harper's Magazine published my article Death of a Mountain, which was a pivotal moment in my career. Initially, the editor was unsure about the story until I focused on one mountain, Lost Mountain, which made the issue more tangible. Harper's gave me a platform to expose the realities of mountaintop removal mining to a broader, urban audience. The magazine played a key role in amplifying environmental concerns in Appalachia.
implicit_text: A well-known magazine published my article about mountaintop removal mining, which was a pivotal moment in my career. Initially, the editor was unsure about the story until I focused on one mountain, which made the issue more tangible. The magazine gave me a platform to expose the realities of mountaintop removal mining to a broader, urban audience. It played a key role in amplifying environmental concerns in Appalachia.

explicit_text: John Dos Passos inspired Wendell Berry's idea to organize a tour for writers to fly over coal fields and talk to people affected by mountaintop removal mining. This was a reinvention of Dos Passos' poverty tours from the 1930s. I participated in this tour, which was effective in raising awareness and pushing back against the coal industry. Dos Passos' legacy influenced how we approached storytelling and activism in Appalachia.
implicit_text: A famous American novelist inspired the idea to organize a tour for writers to fly over coal fields and talk to people affected by mountaintop removal mining. This was a reinvention of his poverty tours from the 1930s. I participated in this tour, which was effective in raising awareness and pushing back against the coal industry. His legacy influenced how we approached storytelling and activism in Appalachia.

explicit_text: Mountaintop removal mining is the environmental issue I have focused much of my writing on, especially in Eastern Kentucky. I wrote extensively about it in Death of a Mountain and my book Lost Mountain, showing how it destroys landscapes and harms communities. The coal industry pushed back hard against our work, often dismissing us as emotional, but we persisted in telling the stories of those affected. This issue became central to my environmental activism and writing.
implicit_text: The environmental issue I have focused much of my writing on involves the removal of mountaintops for coal mining, especially in Eastern Kentucky. I wrote extensively about it in an article and my book, showing how it destroys landscapes and harms communities. The coal industry pushed back hard against our work, often dismissing us as emotional, but we persisted in telling the stories of those affected. This issue became central to my environmental activism and writing.

explicit_text: Teri Blanton was part of the great group of people I met through the fight against mountaintop removal mining. Along with Wendell Berry, Dave Cooper, and others, she was involved in the activism and writing efforts that brought attention to the coal industry's impact. Being around such committed individuals was inspiring, even though I never considered myself a traditional activist.
implicit_text: One activist I met through the fight against mountaintop removal mining was part of a great group of people involved in the activism and writing efforts that brought attention to the coal industry's impact. Being around such committed individuals was inspiring, even though I never considered myself a traditional activist.

explicit_text: Dave Cooper was one of the key people involved in the fight against mountaintop removal mining alongside Wendell Berry, Teri Blanton, and others. We worked together to raise awareness through writing and public engagement. Dave was part of the supportive community that made this environmental activism meaningful to me, even though I preferred writing over direct activism.
implicit_text: Another key person involved in the fight against mountaintop removal mining worked alongside the environmentalist and others. We collaborated to raise awareness through writing and public engagement. He was part of the supportive community that made this environmental activism meaningful to me, even though I preferred writing over direct activism.

explicit_text: Dwight Billings was a significant figure in my academic and activist journey. After I wrote critical op-eds about the University of Kentucky's attitude toward coal, Dwight encouraged me to seek tenure protection. He advocated for me within my department, helping me move from lecturer to assistant professor. Dwight also participated in the environmental community that worked on mountaintop removal issues.
implicit_text: A significant figure in my academic and activist journey encouraged me to seek tenure protection after I wrote critical op-eds about my university's attitude toward coal. He advocated for me within my department, helping me move from lecturer to assistant professor. He also participated in the environmental community that worked on mountaintop removal issues.

explicit_text: Lost Mountain is the book I wrote that grew out of my article Death of a Mountain and my work exposing mountaintop removal mining. I chose the mountain because it was a real place named in mining permits, and it was destroyed within a year. The book was aimed at urban readers to help them understand the environmental and human costs of blowing up mountains for coal. It remains a concise but powerful document of this issue.
implicit_text: The book I wrote grew out of my article about mountaintop removal and my work exposing this mining practice. I chose the mountain because it was a real place named in mining permits, and it was destroyed within a year. The book was aimed at urban readers to help them understand the environmental and human costs of blowing up mountains for coal. It remains a concise but powerful document of this issue.

explicit_text: I went to the Office of Surface Mining to research mining permits, which are very dense and legalistic documents. That's where I found the permit for Lost Mountain, which became the focus of my writing. This research was crucial in grounding my environmental work in actual regulatory and legal realities of coal mining.
implicit_text: I went to a federal office responsible for regulating mining to research mining permits, which are very dense and legalistic documents. That's where I found the permit for the mountain that became the focus of my writing. This research was crucial in grounding my environmental work in actual regulatory and legal realities of coal mining.

explicit_text: Julia Butterfly Hill is an activist I admire who famously sat in a tree for two years to stop logging. I heard her speak once, and she said something that stuck with me: 'find your tree.' That advice helped me focus my own environmental efforts on my mountain and writing. Her example showed me how individual commitment can make a difference in overwhelming issues like climate change.
implicit_text: An activist I admire famously sat in a tree for two years to stop logging. I heard her speak once, and she said something that stuck with me: 'find your tree.' That advice helped me focus my own environmental efforts on my mountain and writing. Her example showed me how individual commitment can make a difference in overwhelming issues like climate change.

explicit_text: Robinson Forest is where I took students to get them excited about nature writing. It was also a place where the reality of strip mining was impossible to ignore, with blasting heard all day. This experience helped me shift from purely nature appreciation to environmental writing focused on preservation and activism. It was a pivotal location in my teaching and thinking about environmental issues.
implicit_text: A forest in Kentucky is where I took students to get them excited about nature writing. This experience helped me shift from purely nature appreciation to environmental writing focused on preservation and activism. It was a pivotal location in my teaching and thinking about environmental issues.

explicit_text: I often refer to Henry David Thoreau's ideas when teaching nature writing, encouraging students to have Thoreauvian experiences in the natural world. His philosophy of simple living and deep connection to nature influenced my early teaching and writing. Over time, I shifted from Thoreau's idealism to a more urgent focus on environmental preservation.
implicit_text: I often refer to a famous 19th-century naturalist's ideas when teaching nature writing, encouraging students to have experiences in the natural world like his. His philosophy of simple living and deep connection to nature influenced my early teaching and writing. Over time, I shifted from his idealism to a more urgent focus on environmental preservation.

explicit_text: E. O. Wilson's concept of the 'naturalist trance' is something I try to help my students experience when we are in places like Robinson Forest. His work on biodiversity and nature inspired me to deepen my engagement with the environment. Wilson's ideas complement Thoreau's and help frame the importance of connecting with nature on a scientific and emotional level.
implicit_text: A renowned biologist's concept of the 'naturalist trance' is something I try to help my students experience when we are in places like the Kentucky forest. His work on biodiversity and nature inspired me to deepen my engagement with the environment. His ideas complement those of the 19th-century naturalist and help frame the importance of connecting with nature on a scientific and emotional level.

explicit_text: I mentioned Donald Trump as an example of someone who might destroy what they love, contrasting him with most people who protect what they care about. This was part of a reflection on human nature and environmental stewardship. Trump represents, in my view, a sociopathic disregard for the environment, which motivates me to tell stories that encourage care and preservation.

Though not deeply elaborated in the interview, Spinoza's ideas about nature and God inform my broader worldview. This philosophical grounding supports my environmental writing and ethics.

explicit_text: I interpret the Kingdom of God, as mentioned in the Gospels, as something present in the natural world around us. This theology, which contrasts with my grandfather's fundamentalist views, motivates me to care for the environment here and now. Seeing the Kingdom of God as before us inspires my environmental writing and activism.

This theology, which contrasts with my grandfather's fundamentalist views, motivates me to care for the environment here and now. Seeing the Kingdom of God as before us inspires my environmental writing and activism.

explicit_text: The Gospels inform my understanding of the Kingdom of God as something present in the here and now, especially in nature. This perspective shapes my environmental ethic and contrasts with my grandfather's belief in a better place after this world. The teachings in the Gospels inspire me to act to preserve the natural world as a manifestation of divine presence.

explicit_text: While I didn't elaborate much on American Gospel in the interview, it relates to my reflections on theology and environmental ethics. It represents a broader cultural and religious context that I engage with when thinking about how faith intersects with care for the earth. My work often challenges traditional views to emphasize stewardship and presence in the natural world.
